1: The LORD's arm is not too short to save, nor his ear too dull to hear. The verse establishes that the problem is not on YHWH's side: his power (arm) has not diminished and his attention (ear) has not faded. The short-arm metaphor addresses the exile community's temptation to conclude that YHWH was defeated by Babylon's gods or had simply become indifferent. Both inferences are explicitly denied before the indictment begins.
2: Your iniquities have created a barrier between you and your God, and your sins have caused him to hide his face so that he does not hear. The partition created by sin is both spatial (a wall between the parties) and relational (averted face). The hidden face is YHWH's withdrawal of the covenant presence — the reversal of the Aaronic blessing's YHWH make his face shine upon you (Numbers 6:25). The exile's silence from heaven is explained as iniquity-induced separation, not divine abandonment.
3: Your hands are stained with blood and your fingers with guilt — your lips have spoken lies and your tongue mutters deceit. The covenant-lawsuit indictment catalogs the body's complicity in covenant violation: hands, fingers, lips, tongue. Blood-guilt was the most serious covenant impurity in Israelite law (Numbers 35:33: blood pollutes the land). The pairing of physical violence (blood) with speech-crime (lies, deceit) covers both the social and legal dimensions of covenant failure.
4: No one calls for justice; no one pleads with integrity — they depend on worthless arguments and speak lies; they conceive evil and give birth to wickedness. The covenant community's judicial system is the indictment's target: the rib (legal plea) process has been corrupted. Conceiving evil and giving birth to wickedness is the agricultural metaphor applied to the moral-legal process — the community produces wickedness as a natural crop. Paul uses this passage (Isaiah 59:7-8) in Romans 3:15-17 as part of his universal-condemnation argument.
5: They hatch the eggs of vipers and weave a spider's web — whoever eats their eggs will die, and if one is cracked, a poisonous snake emerges. The double natural-world image of hatching vipers and weaving webs describes the community's activity as inherently lethal and futile: viper eggs hatch into more vipers, and spider webs trap but provide no genuine shelter. The hatching imagery implies that the evil was incubated and produced over time — systemic corruption, not isolated incidents.
6: Their webs will not serve as clothing — they cannot cover themselves with what they weave. Their works are works of wickedness, and their hands carry out violence. The spider-web as failed clothing captures the self-defeating nature of the wicked community's projects: what they create cannot protect or cover them. In the ANE, clothing was both practical and social — the garment defined status and identity. A covering that cannot cover is both physically useless and socially humiliating.
7: Their feet rush toward evil and they are quick to shed innocent blood — their thoughts are plots of wickedness; ruin and destruction mark their ways. The totality of the body's orientation toward evil — feet running, thoughts plotting — shows the comprehensive moral capture of the community. Paul quotes these verses (Isaiah 59:7-8) in Romans 3:15-17 as universal human condemnation: Their feet are swift to shed blood; ruin and misery are in their paths.
8: They do not know the way of peace — there is no justice in their tracks; they have bent every path crooked, and no one who walks them knows peace. The absence of the way of peace (derekh shalom) is both descriptive and ironic: the covenant community, supposed mediators of YHWH's shalom to the nations, have made every path crooked. No one who walks their bent paths finds the shalom that their covenant vocation was meant to produce and share.
9: Therefore justice is far from us, and righteousness does not reach us — we wait for light, but there is only darkness; for brightness, but we walk in deep shadow. The pronoun shifts from accusation (they, Isaiah 59:3-8) to confession (we, Isaiah 59:9-15): the community owns the indictment. Waiting for light while experiencing darkness is the covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:29 realized: At midday you shall grope as the blind grope in darkness. The community is inside the curse they brought on themselves.
10: We grope along the wall like blind men — we fumble like those with no eyes; we stumble at midday as in the dusk; among the healthy we are like the dead. The blindness-at-noon is a direct echo of the Deuteronomy 28:28-29 covenant curse: YHWH will strike with blindness, causing groping at noon. The community experiencing this curse is simultaneously fulfilling the Mosaic warning and confessing its cause. Among the healthy they are like the dead — the living-dead status of those under covenant judgment.
11: We all growl like bears and moan like doves — we look for justice but there is none; for deliverance but it is far away. Bear-growling and dove-moaning are complementary lament sounds: the bear's aggressive rumble and the dove's plaintive coo. The dove was associated with mourning throughout the ANE (cf. Isaiah 38:14). Looking for mishpat and finding none is the most fundamental covenant failure: the community in which YHWH's justice should be visible has emptied itself of it.
12: For our rebellions are multiplied before you and our sins testify against us — we are deeply aware of our transgressions and we know our own iniquities. The covenant lawsuit reaches its formal confession stage: the defendants admit the evidence. Testify against us uses the covenant-witness language of the suzerainty treaty: the witnesses of the covenant now testify to its violation. Self-awareness of sin is the beginning of the lament's movement toward the divine rescue announced in Isaiah 59:16.
13: We have rebelled against and denied the LORD — we have turned away from following our God; we have spoken oppression and treachery, conceived and repeated from our hearts words of falsehood. The four verbs of apostasy (rebelled, denied, turned away, spoken falsehood) cover the full compass of covenant violation: internal disposition (turning away), speech-act (denying), and habitual pattern (conceiving and repeating). The heart as the source of falsehood parallels Jeremiah 17:9's diagnosis of the human heart as incurably deceitful.
14: Justice has been driven back, and righteousness stands at a distance — truth has fallen in the streets, and honesty cannot get through. The personification of the civic virtues as exiles — justice driven back, righteousness at a distance, truth fallen, honesty unable to enter — is the rhetorical brilliance of the passage. The virtues that should inhabit the covenant community's public life have themselves been expelled: they are now external observers of the community's collapse rather than its operating principles.
15: Truth has vanished, and anyone who turns from evil exposes himself to attack. YHWH saw it and it displeased him that there was no justice. The social inversion is complete: the righteous person is now the dangerous outsider rather than the community norm. YHWH's displeasure (yera' be-einav — it was evil in his eyes) at the absence of mishpat sets up the divine initiative of Isaiah 59:16: when no human champion of justice exists, YHWH himself must act.
16: He saw that there was no man, and was astounded that there was no one to intervene — so his own arm achieved salvation for him, and his own righteousness upheld him. The astonishment at human absence echoes 63:5 and parallels the motif in Isaiah 41:28 and Isaiah 50:2 — YHWH looking for a human partner and finding none. The divine arm achieving salvation alone is the pattern of the Exodus (YHWH fighting for Israel with no human army), now repeated: the new Exodus depends entirely on YHWH's unilateral action. Paul develops this in Romans 11:26 where the Redeemer comes from Zion.
17: He put on righteousness as a breastplate and a helmet of salvation on his head — clothed himself in garments of vengeance and wrapped himself in zeal like a cloak. The divine warrior's armor is entirely his own attributes: righteousness, salvation, vengeance, zeal. This is not conventional ANE military equipment but YHWH's own character serving as his battle gear. Paul adapts this in Ephesians 6:14-17 (the armor of God) and 1 Thessalonians 5:8, where the believer is exhorted to put on the same divine attributes.
18: According to what they have done, so he will repay — fury to his foes, punishment to his enemies; he will pay back even the distant coastlands. The covenant principle of proportional repayment (gemul ke-gemul) governs the divine warrior's action: enemies receive exactly what their deeds warrant. The extension of repayment to the distant coastlands (the ends of the known world) globalizes the divine justice — no enemy of YHWH's covenant purpose escapes his reach.
19: From the west they will fear the name of the LORD and from the east his glory — for he will come like a rushing torrent driven by the Spirit of the LORD. Universal fear of YHWH's name from all compass points follows the divine warrior's comprehensive victory. The rushing torrent (literally: the enemy will come like a river) driven by the Spirit suggests the divine Breath (ruach YHWH) as the propulsive force behind YHWH's redemptive action — the Spirit driving the new Exodus as it had driven the original creation (Genesis 1:2).
20: A Redeemer (go'el) will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from their rebellion. The Redeemer-to-Zion announcement is the climax of the oracle. The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) comes to buy back the alienated family member and property. The qualification — to those who turn from rebellion — introduces the teshuvah condition. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 11:26 as the scriptural warrant for Israel's ultimate salvation: The Deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob.
21: This is my covenant with them: my Spirit that is upon you and my words that I have placed in your mouth shall not leave your mouth, nor the mouth of your children, nor the mouth of your grandchildren — from now on and forever. The new covenant is defined as the permanent indwelling of Spirit and word across three generations and then forever. This is the Jeremiah 31:31-34 and Ezekiel 36:26-27 vision in Second Isaiah's formulation: the internal, permanent, and transmitted presence of YHWH's Spirit and word as the distinguishing mark of the restored covenant community.
